title: Triple chocolate cake
subtitle: Dark, milk and white chocolate in one loaf cake
excerpt: Triple chocolate cake uses all three kinds of chocolate in the same batter: dark for depth, milk for creaminess and white chocolate chips that hold their shape as soft pockets. A single loaf, rich enough to serve in thin slices.
sourceNotes: The triple chocolate combination (dark, milk, white) in a single baked item became popular in British and American bakeries in the 1990s. Incorporating different types of chocolate into a single batter is a technique that appears in British home-baking books from the 2000s onward. This recipe is synthesised from the established British chocolate-cake tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the cooled loaf tightly and freeze for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bakes and keeps well for 3 days at room temperature, well wrapped.

glossaryTerms.definition: Chocolate chips are formulated to hold their shape in the oven rather than melt into the batter. Using chopped bar chocolate instead gives pockets of melted chocolate rather than intact chips. Either works in this recipe. Toss the chips or chopped pieces in a teaspoon of flour before folding in to stop them sinking to the bottom of the tin.

content.text: This is a loaf cake, not a layer cake. Melted dark chocolate goes into the batter directly; milk chocolate is chopped and folded in; white chocolate chips or chopped white chocolate are
added last, coated in a little flour to stop them sinking. The result is a slice that shows all three chocolates at once.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with baking paper. Melt the 100 g dark chocolate in a bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring occasionally. Set aside to cool slightly.

Beat the 100 g softened butter and 180 g light brown sugar until light and creamy. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time, then mix in the melted dark chocolate and 100 g soured cream. Sift over the 200 g flour, 30 g cocoa, 1.5 tsp baking powder and quarter teaspoon salt, and fold until just combined. Toss the 80 g chopped milk chocolate and 80 g white chocolate each in a pinch of flour, then fold both into the batter.

Scrape the batter into the tin. Bake for 45 to 55 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with a few moist crumbs, the top has cracked and the loaf feels set when pressed lightly. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then lift out and transfer to a wire rack for at least 20 minutes before slicing.

items.problem: White and milk chocolate all sank to the bottom
items.solution: Toss the chopped chocolate in a teaspoon of plain flour before folding in. The flour coating provides just enough friction to keep the pieces suspended during baking. Also make sure the batter is not too thin.

The bake was slightly too long or the oven runs hot. The soured cream should keep the crumb moist; an oven thermometer helps confirm the temperature. Remove the loaf as soon as the skewer comes out with a few moist crumbs rather than completely clean.
